one_line_takeaway: A visual-analytics system (Unlearning Comparator) that lets researchers compare machine-unlearning methods across accuracy, efficiency, and MIA-based privacy, introducing a Worst-Case Privacy Score and, via its case study, a new method (Guided Unlearning) that reaches the best WCPS (0.913 vs 0.876 for SCRUB).
key_finding: The visual comparison surfaced behavioral patterns that aggregate metrics hide and directly informed a better method. Standard MIAs are shown to be misleading past zero unlearning accuracy: C-MIA prematurely saturates at 1.0 and E-MIA incorrectly declines, while WCPS increases monotonically toward the retrained distribution (plateauing ~0.8 for FT). Findings identified an 'Elbow Layer' (e.g., layer3.1 in ResNet-18) marking where retain-class CKA bottoms out just before forget-class representations diverge; freezing up to it and re-initializing later layers gave ~30% faster convergence than standard fine-tuning. The resulting novel method, Guided Unlearning (GU) (Warm-Up re-initialization + relabeling to the 2nd-highest logit, tuned Gradient Ascent forgetting, guided fine-tuning recovery), achieved the highest WCPS of 0.913 among all tested methods, beating the prior best of 0.876 (SCRUB), while matching the retrained model's prediction patterns and embeddings and keeping run time competitive.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Unlearning Comparator, a visual analytics (VA) system for the comparative evaluation of Machine Unlearning (MU) methods for image classification. It is primarily an HCI/visualization contribution with a security-relevant privacy-evaluation core. Three concrete deliverables: (1) a design study with MU researchers yielding a four-stage 'Comparative MU Evaluation Workflow' (Build -> Screen -> Contrast -> Attack) distilled from five user tasks (T1-T5); (2) the Unlearning Comparator system itself, which supports side-by-side pairwise comparison of two models across metrics, embeddings, and simulated membership inference attacks (MIAs), at class-, instance-, and layer-levels; and (3) a new Worst-Case Privacy Score (WCPS) and, via a case study, a novel three-stage MU method called Guided Unlearning (GU). Note: FT, RL, GA are prior baselines and SCRUB/SalUn are prior SOTA (cited, Related Work / Section II-B); the paper's OWN novel algorithmic method is GU, and its OWN metric is WCPS. The system, workflow, WCPS, and GU are the authors' contributions; the surveyed MU methods and MIA primitives (C-MIA, E-MIA) are cited prior art the tool operationalizes.
threat_model: The security frame is genuine but scoped to privacy verification, not a real-world exploit. Within the tool, privacy is assessed by simulating membership inference attacks (MIAs) on the forget class: an attacker tries to determine whether specific samples were part of the original training set by inspecting model outputs. The WCPS assumes a deliberately strong/optimal (worst-case, largely infeasible-in-practice) adversary who can access both the predicted label's confidence (log-odds transform) and the entropy of all logit outputs from the unlearned or retrained models, and who compares the unlearned model's output distribution against the retrained (gold-standard) model's distribution to distinguish 'training' vs 'non-training' inputs via threshold decision rules. This is a black-box, distribution-indistinguishability threat model. The authors explicitly note white-box attacks (model-inversion, malicious-unlearning-request attacks on the unlearning process) are out of scope and left to future work. The motivating scenario is GDPR / 'right to be forgotten' compliance and detecting whether residual signals of forgotten data (e.g., a patient's records) remain.
limitations: Scope is narrow: only class-wise unlearning for ten-class image classification (CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST); multi-class, instance-wise, and sub-class unlearning are unsupported. Privacy evaluation is only black-box MIA; white-box, model-inversion, and malicious-unlearning-request attacks are excluded, and the authors concede that defining and accurately estimating a privacy score (including their own distribution-indistinguishability WCPS) remains an open problem. The gold-standard retrained models are precomputed and rarely available in practice; scaling to foundation models/LLMs would require redesign (embedding summaries, token/attention views, alternative retain-only references such as TOFU). The main evaluation is a qualitative case study with only two experts (who also co-designed the system, a possible bias) plus four interviewees; the quantitative validation is a single small ablation table (two forget classes). Embedding view uses only the penultimate layer; 2D UMAP projections can distort high-dimensional structure. The WCPS optimal-adversary assumption is acknowledged as infeasible in practice.

datasets_used: ImageNet (used only as pre-training for the ViT-B/16 backbone)
quantitative_results: GU achieves the highest WCPS of 0.913 among all tested methods, surpassing the previous best of 0.876 (SCRUB)
Table I ablation (forget class 'automobile'), WCPS (higher is better): GA+FT baselines 0.429 -> GATuned+FT 0.467 -> Re-Init+GATuned+FT 0.559 -> full GU 0.743; Retrain gold standard = 1.000
Table I ablation (forget class 'deer'), WCPS: GA+FT 0.663 -> GATuned+FT 0.737 -> Re-Init+GATuned+FT 0.751 -> full GU 0.806; Retrain = 1.000
Full GU accuracy (automobile): UA 0.000 (lower better), RA 0.994, TUA 0.000, TRA 0.938, RT 76.6s; Retrain baseline RT was 3524.7s (i.e., GU runs within ~3% of full-retraining time, ~46x faster)
Full GU (deer): UA 0.000, RA 0.994, TUA 0.000, TRA 0.944, RT 76.6s; Retrain RT 3513.5s
Freezing up to the Elbow Layer and re-initializing subsequent layers gave ~30% faster convergence vs standard fine-tuning
WCPS for FT plateaus around 0.8 (not 1.0) as epochs progress, while C-MIA saturates at 1.0 by epoch 1 (where WCPS is only ~0.4) and E-MIA erroneously declines
Layer-wise CKA example: at internal layer layer4.0, Model A drops to 0.246 while Model B stays at 0.822 (Model B still encodes forget-class features)
WCPS computed over 100 evenly spaced thresholds x {confidence, entropy}, entropy temperature T=2.0
Runtime: unlearning takes seconds to ~5 min on one NVIDIA A6000 GPU; eval pipeline (CKA + dim-reduction) ~30s

User study scale: design study with 2 MU experts over 2 months; case study over 1 week; 4 additional interviewees (E1-E4), 70-90 min sessions; E3 estimated the tool covers ~80% of necessary evaluation steps
baselines_compared: Retrain from scratch (gold-standard reference, not an efficient method)
SalUn / Saliency Unlearning - prior SOTA (weight-saliency masking + random labeling)
Standard C-MIA and E-MIA (prior MIA baselines the WCPS metric is contrasted against)
